column1: What is BCS and why does field order matter?;BCS (Binary Canonical Serialization) encodes Move structs into a deterministic byte vector. Fields are serialized in exact struct definition order. The encoding is NOT self-describing — no field names
column3: just raw bytes concatenated. Decoder must peel fields in the same order they were defined. Source: Move Book §8.17 BCS — move-book.com/programmability/bcs;sui::bcs::field_order

column1: Common mistake: "If I decode BCS fields in the wrong order
column2: the decoder will abort with an error." Is this correct?;❌ No. BCS has no schema. The decoder reads bytes blindly. Wrong peel order → silent data corruption (garbage values)
column3: NOT an error. It may only abort if a length prefix decodes to an impossibly large value — not guaranteed. This makes BCS mismatches dangerous: code compiles and runs
column4: but produces wrong data. Source: Move Book §8.17 BCS;sui::bcs::silent_corruption

column1: How do you encode and decode a struct with BCS in Move?;Encode: bcs::to_bytes(&my_struct) → vector<u8>. Decode: let mut decoder = bcs::new(bytes); then peel fields in struct definition order — decoder.peel_address()
column3: etc. Each peel call advances the cursor. Source: Move Book §8.17 BCS — move-book.com/programmability/bcs;sui::bcs::encode_decode

column1: What are the four hash functions available on Sui and when do you use each?;std::hash: sha2_256(bytes: vector<u8>)
column2: sha3_256(bytes: vector<u8>) — takes owned bytes. sui::hash: keccak256(&bytes)
column3: blake2b256(&bytes) — takes reference. Selection rule: match what the off-chain signer uses. keccak256 for EVM interop; sha2_256 for standard off-chain systems; sha3_256 for OpenSSL-default systems; blake2b256 for performance. All are cryptographically adequate. Source: Sui Docs > Cryptography > Hashing;sui::hash::selection_rules

column1: Common mistake: "Any hash function works — they're all equally secure for Sui sig verification." Is this correct?;❌ Misleading. Collision resistance is similar
column2: but the hash function on-chain must match the one the off-chain signer used. Mismatch → different digest → ed25519_verify always returns false. The code will compile and run
column3: but every verification fails silently. Rule: ask "what does the signer hash with?" and use the same function on-chain. Source: Sui Docs > Cryptography > Hashing;sui::hash::mismatch

column1: What is the complete on-chain signature verification flow on Sui?;1. Off-chain: serialize permit struct to BCS bytes → hash → sign with private key → 64-byte Ed25519 sig. 2. On-chain: re-encode same struct to BCS bytes → re-hash → call ed25519_verify(&sig
column4: E_INVALID_SIG). The contract holds the public key
column5: never the private key. Verification confirms signer owned the private key paired with the known pubkey. Source: Sui Docs > Cryptography > Signing — docs.sui.io/guides/developer/cryptography/signing;sui::sig::verification_flow

column1: How does Sui signature verification differ from Ethereum's ecrecover?;Sui: ed25519_verify(&sig
column3: &hash) → bool. You provide the expected public key; function returns true/false. Algorithm: Ed25519. Ethereum: ecrecover(hash
column6: s) → address. Function recovers the signer's address from the signature; no pubkey input needed. Algorithm: ECDSA secp256k1. Key difference: Sui requires knowing whose key to check; Ethereum recovers the key. Source: Sui Docs > Cryptography > Signing;sui::sig::vs_ethereum

column1: What is a replay attack in the context of permit-based authorization?;A replay attack reuses a valid signature in a context the signer didn't intend — same function called again (double-mint)
column2: or same sig used in a different function (mint permit used to burn). Defenses required: (1) nonce or used-sig table — one-time-use; (2) expiry timestamp — invalid after deadline; (3) domain separation — sig binds to one specific function/contract. Source: Sui Docs > Cryptography > Signing;sui::sig::replay_attack

column1: What is domain separation and why is it needed in permit patterns?;Domain separation adds a function-specific or contract-specific tag to the signed message. Without it
column2: a signature for mint_with_permit is cryptographically identical to a signature for burn_with_permit if both use the same struct and key — the attacker can cross-submit. Fix: add action: vector<u8> (e.g.
column3: b"mint" or b"burn") to the permit struct
column4: included in the signed bytes. Source: Sui Docs > Cryptography > Signing;sui::sig::domain_separation

column1: Security check: A permit has no expiry field. What attack does this enable and what is the severity?;MEDIUM severity. If the admin signs a permit today and their key is compromised later
column2: or if they accidentally sign a malicious permit
column3: that signature is valid forever. No on-chain way to revoke it. Fix: add expires_at: u64 to the permit struct; on-chain assert(tx_context::epoch(ctx) <= permit.expires_at
column4: E_EXPIRED). Source: Sui Docs > Cryptography > Signing;sui::sig::no_expiry

column1: Security check: burn_with_permit verifies a permit signature but then burns whatever coin is passed without checking coin::value == permit.amount. What is the vulnerability?;HIGH severity. The signature proves the admin approved a burn of N tokens
column2: but the contract doesn't enforce that the coin being burned equals N. An attacker with a valid burn permit for 1 token can use it to burn 10
column5: E_AMOUNT_MISMATCH) before burning. Source: Sui Docs > Cryptography > Signing;sui::sig::amount_not_enforced

column1: Security check: A bridge message has from_chain and to_chain fields but no nonce. A valid message is signed by the relayer. What attack does this enable?;CRITICAL: Double-submission replay. from_chain + to_chain prevent replaying the message on the wrong chain
column2: but the same valid message can be submitted multiple times on the correct chain. Each submission releases assets. Fix: add nonce: u64 (or message_id) to the message; maintain a Table<u64
column3: bool> of consumed nonces on-chain; assert the nonce hasn't been used
column4: then mark it. This is the mechanism behind most bridge hacks. Source: Sui Docs > Cryptography > Signing;sui::sig::bridge_nonce

column1: Common mistake: "Move's safety model prevents cross-contract replay attacks automatically." Is this correct?;❌ Partially wrong. Move prevents reentrancy (no callbacks mid-execution) — that's real. But cross-contract replay is NOT prevented automatically. If two contracts accept the same permit struct signed by the same key
column2: a permit for contract A is valid for contract B. Domain separation must include a contract address or module identifier in the signed struct. Source: Sui Docs > Cryptography > Signing;sui::sig::cross_contract_replay
